name: Chhaya Community Development Corporation
description_short: Chhaya CDC is a 501(c)(3) non-profit providing free tax preparation, housing advocacy, economic justice programs, and financial counseling to South Asian and Indo-Caribbean communities in NYC.
description_long:
Chhaya Community Development Corporation was founded to address the housing and economic needs of low-income South Asian and Indo-Caribbean New Yorkers. For over 20 years, the organization has served tens of thousands of individuals across New York City with a dual approach: direct services combined with systemic advocacy. The organization operates from two locations—Jackson Heights and Richmond Hill—both neighborhoods with significant South Asian and Indo-Caribbean populations.

Chhaya's primary offerings include: free tax preparation services (2026 program currently active), housing justice advocacy and tenant organizing, economic justice programs including homeownership workshops and first-time homebuyer support, asset-building peer lending circles for credit establishment, small business support and legal defense, immigration rights protection and integration services, and civic engagement programs. They also offer free financial counseling and connect clients with legal resources. The organization explicitly targets immigrants with limited English proficiency and low-income households.

Chhaya distinguishes itself through culturally competent, community-rooted organizing rather than transactional service delivery. They combine direct assistance (like helping tenants report housing violations or small business owners appeal fines) with leadership development and systemic advocacy (organizing with elected officials, tenant unions, and community groups). Their peer lending circles and asset-building programs are designed specifically for individuals without traditional credit histories—a common barrier for immigrant communities. The organization prioritizes self-determination and resident power-building over one-way aid.

The honest assessment: Chhaya is a legitimate, established non-profit with clear community impact, but their free services are community-specific (targeting South Asian and Indo-Caribbean populations) and geographically limited to NYC. They are not a national resource and their capacity is constrained by non-profit funding. Their programs are free, but availability depends on eligibility screening and likely has waitlists given the scope of community need they serve.
pros: Completely free tax preparation services with no hidden fees or sales pitch
Free peer lending circles specifically designed for people building or rebuilding credit without traditional credit history
Culturally competent staff working with South Asian and Indo-Caribbean communities in their native context
Combines direct financial services with legal advocacy (e.g., helping small business owners appeal fines)
Free first-time homebuyer workshops and homeownership program support
Free housing counseling and tenant rights education in multiple languages for immigrants with limited English
Established 501(c)(3) non-profit with 20+ years operating history and two physical locations
cons: Services limited to South Asian and Indo-Caribbean communities—not available to general population
Geographic service area restricted to New York City only (Jackson Heights and Richmond Hill centers)
As a non-profit with limited capacity, programs likely have waitlists and eligibility screening requirements
No online application or remote service option mentioned; requires in-person visits to physical offices
Limited information about response times, appointment availability, or specific eligibility criteria on website
best_for: South Asian and Indo-Caribbean immigrants in NYC seeking free tax filing assistance
First-generation homebuyers without traditional credit history looking to build credit and save for a down payment
Small business owners in NYC immigrant communities facing regulatory fines or compliance issues
Tenants in NYC facing housing code violations or landlord disputes seeking rights education and organizing support
diagnosis: Chhaya is best for South Asian and Indo-Caribbean immigrants in New York City seeking free, culturally competent financial and housing services. The main caveat is that services are community and geographically restricted—if you don't fit the target demographic or live outside NYC, you will not be eligible.
services: Asset-building peer lending circles for credit establishment
Tenant rights education and housing code violation reporting support
Landlord accountability organizing and tenant union coordination
Small business legal defense and regulatory appeal support
Immigration rights protection and immigrant integration services
Free financial counseling and credit guidance
Civic engagement and community organizing programs
Connection to pro-bono legal services (TakeRoot Justice partnership mentioned)
typical_results_timeline: Counseling available within 1-2 weeks of contact.
